lessons.title: Clapping in a Canyon: Pulses, Listening, and a Sneaky Far Echo
lessons.content:
You already know a radar sends out a radio wave and times the echo that bounces back: a longer wait means a farther target. But here is something we glossed over. A pulsed radar does not send **one** pulse and stop. It sends a short pulse, then listens, then sends another, then listens, again and again, **many times every second**. The name for how many pulses it sends per second is the **PRF (Pulse Repetition Frequency)** — literally 'how often the pulse repeats'.

Think of standing in a canyon and **clapping over and over**. After each clap you listen for the echo before you clap again. PRF is just how fast you clap. Between claps, you are quiet and listening.

Now the catch. Suppose a cliff is very far away, so its echo takes a long time to come back. If you clap again **before** that far echo returns, the late echo arrives **after** your next clap — and now you cannot tell which clap it belongs to. Your ear assumes it came from the most recent clap, so the faraway cliff sounds **close**. Radar has the exact same problem: a distant target's echo can arrive after the next pulse has already gone out, so it looks nearer than it really is. Engineers call this a 'second-time-around' echo, and the confusion is called **range ambiguity**.

The fix is simple: **wait longer between pulses** so even far echoes get home before the next pulse leaves. The farthest distance a radar can measure without this confusion is its **maximum unambiguous range**. Clapping faster (higher PRF) leaves less waiting time, so it **shrinks** that range.

In short: a radar fires pulses repeatedly (PRF = pulses per second) and listens in the gaps; if a far echo comes back after the next pulse, it looks deceptively close, so a higher PRF gives a shorter maximum unambiguous range.

items.prompt: What does PRF (Pulse Repetition Frequency) tell you?
choices: How many pulses the radar sends out per second
How tall the radar antenna is
What colour the target appears on the screen
How loud the radar's echo sounds to a person
items.explanation: PRF means 'how often the pulse repeats', that is, how many pulses the radar transmits each second. It has nothing to do with antenna height, on-screen colour, or audible loudness; a radar simply fires pulse after pulse, and PRF counts them per second.

items.prompt: A pulsed radar does what between sending one pulse and the next?
choices: It shuts down completely until the operator restarts it
It listens for returning echoes
It sends a steady, never-ending wave
It changes its physical location
items.explanation: Between pulses the radar goes quiet and listens for the echoes coming back, just like pausing to hear the echo after a clap. It does not power down, move, or transmit continuously; the listening gap is when echoes are received.

items.prompt: Clapping repeatedly in a canyon and listening for each echo before the next clap is a good everyday picture of:
choices: The colour of a radar screen
How a radar sends pulses over and over and listens in between
Why metal reflects radio waves
How heavy a radar dish is
items.explanation: Clapping again and again, listening for each echo in the gaps, mirrors how a pulsed radar fires pulse after pulse and listens between them. The analogy is about the repeated send-and-listen rhythm, not screen colour, reflection, or weight.

items.prompt: Recap from before: how does a radar work out how far away a target is?
choices: By weighing the returning echo
By counting how many pulses it has ever sent
From how long the echo takes to come back (the round-trip time)
From the colour of the target
items.explanation: Distance comes from timing the echo: a longer round trip means a farther target, exactly as in the earlier sub-topic. Echoes are not weighed, total pulse counts do not give distance, and radar cannot see colour.

items.prompt: Why can a very distant target end up looking CLOSER than it really is?
choices: Its echo speeds up on the way back
Far targets always send stronger echoes
Its echo gets brighter with distance
Its echo returns after the next pulse went out, so the radar links it to the wrong pulse
items.explanation: A faraway echo can arrive after the radar has already sent its next pulse, so the radar assumes it belongs to that recent pulse and reads a short round trip, making the target look close. This 'second-time-around' echo is not about waves speeding up, strengthening, or brightening with distance.

items.prompt: What sets a radar's MAXIMUM UNAMBIGUOUS RANGE (the farthest it can measure without confusion)?
choices: How long it waits between pulses before sending the next one
The colour of the radar's paint
How many people are watching the screen
How heavy the antenna is
items.explanation: The waiting time between pulses decides how far an echo can travel and still get home before the next pulse leaves; that distance is the maximum unambiguous range. Paint, audience size, and weight have nothing to do with it.

items.prompt: A friend says: 'A higher PRF is always better.' Why is that not true for measuring range?
choices: Higher PRF makes the radar heavier
Higher PRF leaves less waiting time between pulses, which SHORTENS the maximum unambiguous range
Higher PRF turns the echo into sound
Higher PRF has no effect on anything at all
items.explanation: Clapping faster (higher PRF) leaves less time to hear far echoes cleanly, so it shrinks the maximum unambiguous range and makes distant targets more likely to look falsely close. Higher PRF is a trade-off, not a free win; it does not change weight or turn echoes into sound.

items.prompt: In the canyon, how do you make sure even a far cliff's echo is heard cleanly?
choices: Clap much faster
Clap more quietly
Wait longer between claps so the far echo returns before you clap again
Face a different direction
items.explanation: Waiting longer between claps gives the distant echo time to come back before the next clap, so you can tell which clap it belongs to. For radar this means a lower PRF (longer gaps) gives a larger unambiguous range. Clapping faster or quieter, or turning, does not solve the timing confusion.

items.prompt: A 'second-time-around' echo is best described as:
choices: An echo so weak the radar never hears it
An echo from a far target that arrives after the next pulse, so it is mistaken for a nearby target
The second radar in a pair of radars
An echo that travels around the world twice
items.explanation: A second-time-around echo comes from a distant target but arrives after the radar has sent its next pulse, so the radar ties it to that newer pulse and reads it as close. It is not about a weak echo, a second radar, or circling the globe.

lessons.title: Two Blips or One Blob? Sharp Pulses and the Long Look
lessons.content:
Detecting a target is one thing. Telling **two** nearby targets apart is another. **Range resolution** is the answer to: how close can two objects be and still show up as **two** separate things, not one merged blob?

Here is the intuition. Imagine two people standing close together and you shout once. If your shout is a quick, **sharp clap**, you hear two distinct echoes — one from each person. If instead you let out a long, smeared 'aaah', the two echoes overlap and blur into one muddy sound. A **short, sharp pulse separates close echoes; a long, smeared pulse blurs them together.**

So what really sets resolution? The pulse's **bandwidth** — a measure of how 'sharp' or sudden the pulse is in terms of frequency content. A **wider bandwidth** (a crisper, more sudden pulse) resolves **finer** detail. Put plainly: **finer range resolution needs more bandwidth.** Beware a common trap: it is *not* simply that a longer pulse is better — if anything a long, smeared pulse blurs detail. (Clever tricks let a long pulse *act* sharp, called 'pulse compression'; that comes in the waveforms sub-topic. For now: sharper / wider-bandwidth = finer range resolution.)

The same 'sharpness' idea shows up for **speed**. Recall a radar can also measure how fast a target moves toward or away, using the **Doppler** effect (the pitch-shift you hear as a siren passes). To measure speed **finely**, the radar must watch the target for a **longer stretch of time** — a longer 'look', sometimes called the coherent processing interval. A **longer look gives finer speed resolution**, the way watching a runner for several seconds tells you their pace better than a single glance.

In short: range resolution (splitting two close targets) is set by **bandwidth / pulse sharpness**, not just pulse length — wider bandwidth means finer detail; and finer **speed** resolution comes from watching the target for a longer time.

items.prompt: What does 'range resolution' describe?
choices: How far away the radar can detect anything at all
How close two targets can be and still show up as two separate things, not one blob
How many pulses the radar sends per second
How heavy the target is
items.explanation: Range resolution is about separating two nearby targets into two distinct returns rather than one merged blob. It is different from maximum detection range (how far you can see at all) and from PRF (pulses per second), and it has nothing to do with weight.

items.prompt: What mainly sets a radar's range resolution?
choices: The pulse's bandwidth (how sharp or sudden it is)
The colour of the radar screen
The number of operators on duty
The weight of the antenna
items.explanation: Range resolution is set by the pulse's bandwidth: a sharper, wider-bandwidth pulse resolves finer detail. Screen colour, staffing, and antenna weight do not determine how finely two targets can be separated.

items.prompt: To resolve FINER detail (separate two targets that are very close), a radar needs:
choices: Less bandwidth (a more smeared pulse)
More bandwidth (a sharper, crisper pulse)
A heavier antenna
A brighter screen colour
items.explanation: Finer range resolution needs MORE bandwidth, meaning a sharper, more sudden pulse that separates close echoes. Less bandwidth means a smeared pulse that blurs them together, and weight or colour do not affect resolution.

items.prompt: A beginner says: 'A longer pulse always gives better range resolution.' Why is this wrong?
choices: Because longer pulses are too expensive
Because pulse length has no connection to radar at all
Because a long, smeared pulse tends to BLUR two close echoes together; sharpness (bandwidth) is what resolves them
Because longer pulses make the radar lighter
items.explanation: A long, smeared pulse blurs nearby echoes into one blob, so simply making the pulse longer does not improve resolution; bandwidth (sharpness) is the real driver. (Pulse compression can make a long pulse act sharp, but that is a separate trick.) Cost and weight are not the point.

items.prompt: Two people stand close together. Which 'shout' lets you hear them as two separate echoes?
choices: A long, drawn-out 'aaah'
A quick, sharp clap
A whisper
Saying nothing at all
items.explanation: A quick, sharp clap produces two crisp, separate echoes, just as a sharp (wide-bandwidth) radar pulse resolves two close targets. A long smeared sound blurs the echoes together, and a whisper or silence does not help you separate them.

items.prompt: On the radar's display, what happens to two close targets when the pulse is long and smeared?
choices: They merge into one blob
They split into three blips
They disappear entirely
They swap places
items.explanation: A long, smeared pulse blurs the two echoes together so they appear as a single blob, which is exactly poor range resolution. They do not multiply into three, vanish, or swap places.

items.prompt: Besides distance, a radar can also measure a target's speed using which effect?
choices: The Doppler effect (a frequency shift, like a siren changing pitch as it passes)
The colour of the target
The target's weight
The brightness of the screen
items.explanation: Radar measures how fast a target moves toward or away using the Doppler effect, the same frequency shift you hear when a siren's pitch changes as it passes. Colour, weight, and screen brightness do not carry speed information.

items.prompt: To measure a target's SPEED more finely, what should the radar do?
choices: Watch the target for a longer stretch of time (a longer 'look')
Glance at the target for the shortest possible instant
Paint the antenna a different colour
Add more weight to the dish
items.explanation: Finer speed resolution comes from watching the target over a longer time (a longer look, or coherent processing interval), like watching a runner for several seconds to judge their pace. A single instant gives a poorer speed estimate, and colour or weight are irrelevant.

items.prompt: Watching a runner for several seconds to judge their pace better than a single glance is the best everyday picture of:
choices: Why metal reflects radio waves
Range ambiguity
A longer 'look' giving finer speed resolution
How heavy the antenna is
items.explanation: Observing the runner longer to judge pace mirrors how a longer look (coherent processing interval) gives a radar finer speed resolution. It is not about reflection, range ambiguity (a far echo arriving late), or weight.

items.prompt: Which pair of ideas is matched correctly?
choices: Finer range detail → wider bandwidth (sharper pulse); finer speed detail → longer look
Finer range detail → longer look; finer speed detail → wider bandwidth
Both finer range and finer speed come only from a heavier antenna
Both finer range and finer speed come only from screen colour
items.explanation: Sharper, wider-bandwidth pulses give finer range resolution, while a longer look (more time watching) gives finer speed resolution. Swapping these two, or blaming antenna weight or screen colour, gets the cause-and-effect wrong.

lessons.title: You Can't Win Both: The PRF Dilemma and the Range-Doppler Map
lessons.content:
We have met two kinds of confusion. **Range** can be ambiguous: clap too fast (high PRF) and a far echo looks close. It turns out **speed** can be ambiguous too: just as range wraps around, a target's measured speed can fold back on itself, and the PRF sets a **maximum unambiguous speed** the same way it sets a maximum unambiguous range.

Here is the twist that makes radar hard, and it is worth saying slowly. The two problems pull in **opposite** directions:

- A **LOW PRF** (long gaps between pulses) gives plenty of time for far echoes to return → **clean, unambiguous range** — but it samples the target too sparsely to pin down fast motion → **ambiguous speed**.
- A **HIGH PRF** (short gaps) samples motion often → **clean, unambiguous speed** — but leaves no time for far echoes → **ambiguous range**.

So with a single PRF you **cannot** be perfectly unambiguous in **both** range and speed at once. This is the **PRF dilemma** — like one volume knob that can't make both the bass and the treble perfect at the same time. Real radars **compromise** (a medium PRF that is 'okay' at both) or cleverly **vary / stagger** the PRF — firing at a few different rates and combining the results to untangle the true range and speed.

Finally, where does all this end up? Radars lay their results on a simple **2D grid called a range-Doppler map**: distance runs along one axis and speed (Doppler) along the other. Every target shows up as a **bright spot** sitting at its range and its speed — a slow far plane and a fast near car land in different places. It is the natural picture a radar paints, and we will explore it in depth later.

In short: PRF limits both range and speed, but in opposite ways — low PRF is range-clean / speed-ambiguous and high PRF is speed-clean / range-ambiguous, so radars compromise or stagger the PRF, and they display the answer as bright spots on a range-Doppler map.

items.prompt: What is the 'PRF dilemma'?
choices: A single PRF cannot give clean (unambiguous) range AND clean speed at the same time
Radars cannot send more than one pulse
Higher PRF always makes everything better
A radar can never measure speed at all
items.explanation: The PRF dilemma is that one PRF setting trades off the two confusions: you can be unambiguous in range or in speed, but not perfectly in both at once. Radars do send many pulses and can measure speed; the point is that a single PRF cannot win both, and higher PRF is not a universal improvement.

items.prompt: A LOW PRF (long gaps between pulses) gives you:
choices: Ambiguous range but clean speed
Clean range and clean speed at the same time
Neither range nor speed information
Clean (unambiguous) range but ambiguous speed
items.explanation: Long gaps let far echoes return cleanly, so range is unambiguous, but the target is sampled too sparsely to pin down fast motion, so speed becomes ambiguous. A low PRF cannot give both cleanly (that is the dilemma), and it certainly still provides range information.

items.prompt: A HIGH PRF (short gaps between pulses) gives you:
choices: Clean range and clean speed at the same time
Ambiguous range and ambiguous speed
Clean (unambiguous) speed but ambiguous range
Clean (unambiguous) range but ambiguous speed
items.explanation: Short gaps sample the target's motion often, giving clean speed, but leave too little time for far echoes, so range becomes ambiguous. This is the mirror image of low PRF; you still cannot get both clean at once.

items.prompt: A friend insists: 'Just pick the one perfect PRF that is unambiguous in both range and speed.' What is the problem?
choices: PRF has nothing to do with range or speed
Such a single perfect PRF does not exist; clean range and clean speed pull in opposite directions
Only very old radars have this problem
Speed cannot be measured by radar at all
items.explanation: Because long gaps help range while short gaps help speed, no single PRF is unambiguous in both at once, which is the whole dilemma. PRF very much affects both, the issue is fundamental (not just old radars), and radar can measure speed.

items.prompt: How do real radars deal with the PRF dilemma?
choices: They give up on measuring speed
They use a medium PRF compromise, or vary/stagger the PRF and combine the results
They always use the highest PRF possible
They turn the radar off and on repeatedly
items.explanation: Radars either pick a medium PRF that is acceptable at both range and speed, or fire at several different PRFs and combine the results to untangle the true range and speed. They do not abandon speed, blindly maximize PRF, or solve it by power-cycling.

items.prompt: One volume knob that cannot make both the bass and the treble perfect at once is a good everyday picture of:
choices: The PRF dilemma: one setting can't be best at two opposing things
Why radar uses radio waves instead of light
How echo timing gives distance
Why metal reflects radar
items.explanation: A single knob that cannot perfect two opposing things at once captures the PRF dilemma, where one PRF cannot be unambiguous in both range and speed. It is not about wave choice, echo timing, or reflection.

items.prompt: Besides range, what else can be AMBIGUOUS, with its maximum set by the PRF?
choices: The target's colour
The target's measured speed
The radar's weight
The number of operators
items.explanation: Just as PRF sets a maximum unambiguous range, it also sets a maximum unambiguous speed; beyond it, a target's measured speed folds back on itself. Colour, weight, and staffing are not quantities that wrap around with PRF.

items.prompt: What is a range-Doppler map?
choices: A list of every pulse the radar has ever sent
A 2D grid with range on one axis and speed (Doppler) on the other, where targets appear as bright spots
A paper map of the countryside around the radar
A photograph of the radar's antenna
items.explanation: A range-Doppler map is a 2D grid with distance along one axis and speed (Doppler) along the other; each target shows up as a bright spot at its range and speed. It is not a pulse log, a geographic map, or a photo of hardware.

items.prompt: On a range-Doppler map, how does a target appear?
choices: As a written label spelling out its name
As a sound the operator hears
It does not appear; the map is always blank
As a bright spot located at its range and its speed
items.explanation: Each target shows up as a bright spot positioned by its range (one axis) and its speed (the other axis), so a slow far plane and a fast near car land in different places. It is not a text label, a sound, and the map is not blank.

items.prompt: A slow, faraway plane and a fast, nearby car would appear on a range-Doppler map:
choices: In exactly the same spot
At different spots, because they differ in both range and speed
Only if they are the same colour
Never, because radar cannot show two targets
items.explanation: Because the two axes are range and speed, two targets that differ in distance and speed land at different spots on the map. Position does not depend on colour, and a range-Doppler map readily shows many targets at once.
